questions.question: You are gathering requirements for a new feature in a customer management system. The product owner has given a user interface feature as the next highest priority and you are collocated with the team receiving the benefits. What will you do next?
options: A. Show the customer team how the new user interface will look as they will be using it
B. Communicate the requirements to developers with a coherent logical flow of ideas
C. Elicit requirements into user stories with clear, verifiable, and traceable acceptance criteria
D. Show the mockup or prototype to the developers and ask them to develop based on that
questions.explanation: Well documented requirements are clear, concise, verifiable, consistent, complete, and traceable. Eliciting requirements into user stories with acceptance criteria is the best practice for ensuring clarity and traceability. While prototypes are useful, they may not provide enough information for developers without well-defined acceptance criteria.

questions.question: Your agile team has created a scope statement and a work breakdown structure but is unsure how it will work with Sprints and user stories. What will you do next?
options: A. Assign activities to each of the scope items in the work breakdown structure and place them on a Gantt chart
B. Create a product road map with the team using the lowest most detailed part of the work breakdown structure
C. Group the work by large themes of customer value shown as epics and then break them down into smaller user stories to complete in each Sprint
D. Ask the team to trace the requirements properly using a requirements traceability matrix
questions.explanation: In agile, work is organized by epics (large themes of customer value) which are then decomposed into smaller user stories that can be completed within a Sprint. This is the agile equivalent of work breakdown structure decomposition. Product road maps are typically feature-level, not the most detailed level, and Gantt charts are traditional project management tools.

questions.question: Your project team is trialing an agile way of work. The product owner has a clear vision for the product, but there is confusion in the team as to how much work should be done on a user story before it is taken to the Sprint review and how they will know that a story is complete. What will you do next?
options: A. Refer to the user story acceptance criteria and your team's definition of done
B. Review the user story with multiple users to ensure it is correct
C. Ask the product owner to sign off on the completed user story
D. Ensure quality testing on the user story is completed and passed
questions.explanation: The definition of done specifies when work is complete. Acceptance criteria and the definition of done collectively ensure clarity on completion. While product owner sign-off and quality testing are part of the definition of done, the most direct answer is to establish and refer to the acceptance criteria and definition of done framework.
questions.topic: Definition of Done and Acceptance Criteria

questions.question: You have been working closely with the quality manager of your project planning the budget for the project phase that is due to begin in the next two months. The quality manager mentions the need to budget for appraisal costs to avoid quality issues in the future. What will you do next?
options: A. Ensure there is enough budget for training to prevent quality issues
B. Ensure there is enough budget for a quality tester to verify the deliverables against the agreed specifications
C. Ensure there is enough budget to create a quality assurance plan
D. Ensure there is enough budget to allow for rework or rectification when defects are found
questions.explanation: Appraisal costs are verification and audit activities such as testing. Option B directly addresses appraisal costs by budgeting for quality testers to verify deliverables. Training is a prevention cost, rework is a failure cost, and creating a QA plan is a planning activity, not an appraisal cost.

questions.question: Your agile project has recently had multiple quality issues. Project features have been prioritized by the product owner and broken down into user stories. What will you do to help reduce the cost of quality issues the most?
options: A. Quality test each user story according to its acceptance criteria before release
B. Advise the team that any quality defects will come out of their bonus
C. Perform testing all in one go at the end to reduce the cost of testing resources
D. Ensure a triad of developer, tester, and customer is involved during requirements and design
questions.explanation: The cost of change is least during requirements and design (20 times more costly during build, 50 times during test, 150 times in production). By involving developers, testers, and customers from the very beginning, defects are caught early when they are least expensive to fix. While testing acceptance criteria is important, catching issues early through collaborative involvement reduces cost the most.
questions.topic: Cost of Quality and Early Defect Detection
